title: Vanilla Swiss roll
subtitle: A fatless sponge rolled around whipped cream
excerpt: A light, fatless sponge baked thin in a Swiss roll tin and rolled while still warm around a layer of whipped cream. No fat in the sponge itself. Makes one roll, 8 slices.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the Swiss roll documented as a rolled sponge cake filled with jam or cream. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the same rolled sponge appears across the British household baking entries as a standard afternoon tea item. The fatless sponge method is older than the butter-enriched genoise and is the traditional form recorded in nineteenth-century British household manuals.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The unfilled sponge can be rolled in a clean tea towel (without filling) and left for up to 4 hours. Fill and re-roll just before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 210°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A sponge cake made with no butter or oil. Volume comes entirely from the air beaten into the eggs and sugar. The result is lighter and drier than a butter sponge and requires quick, careful folding of the flour to preserve the air. Fatless sponges bake faster and are more fragile when handled warm — which is also why they can be rolled before cooling.

content.text: The Swiss roll works because a

is pliable when warm. The fat in a standard Victoria sponge would make it crack. Roll the sponge immediately after it comes from the oven, before it stiffens. If it cracks at the edges, that is normal and will be hidden in the roll.

Roll it first in a clean tea towel with a sheet of baking paper dusted with caster sugar. Once cooled, unroll, add the cream, and re-roll. The initial rolling sets the muscle memory of the sponge and means the final roll will not crack.
Make and bake the sponge — 10–12 minutes

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Grease and line a 33 × 23 cm Swiss roll tin with baking paper. Whisk the {{eggs}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} together with a hand mixer for 5 minutes, until the mixture is very pale, thick, and has tripled in volume. The whisk should leave a ribbon trail on the surface for 3 seconds. Sift the {{plain-flour}} over the surface in three additions, folding each in with a large metal spoon using as few strokes as possible. Pour into the tin and tilt to level.
Bake for 10–12 minutes, until the sponge is pale gold, springy to the touch, and has pulled away slightly from the edges.

While the sponge bakes, lay a clean tea towel on the worksurface and top it with a sheet of baking paper dusted generously with caster sugar. As soon as the sponge comes out of the oven, turn it out onto the sugared paper. Peel off the lining paper. Using the tea towel as a guide, roll the sponge up tightly from the short end with the paper inside. Leave to cool completely. When cold, unroll, spread with {{jam}} if using, then the whipped {{double-cream}}. Roll up again, this time without the paper.
